impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act when the unpatented products have substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under federal antitrust laws. Ingevity conditioned licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' agreements to exclusively purchase Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the jury's finding that the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses, defeating Ingevity's defense that its products were nonstaple goods protected from antitrust liability.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors like BASF gain protection against patent holders who leverage patent rights to foreclose competition in adjacent unpatented product markets.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face significant antitrust liability risk when tying unpatented product purchases to patent licenses, even where the patent covers some uses of the tied product. Companies with dominant patents must carefully structure licensing to avoid tying claims.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact preclude summary judgment on inequitable conduct in patent prosecution and Walker Process antitrust fraud claims based on alleged concealment of prior art.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud claims, finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. Tenaris allegedly concealed prior CYMAX documents when obtaining patents on its quench-and-temper coiled tubing product. The court held that factual disputes about intent to deceive and the materiality of the withheld prior art precluded summary judgment for either party.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent challengers benefit from the court's willingness to send both inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud claims to trial, providing a pathway to hold patent holders accountable for prosecution misconduct.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face increased litigation risk where prior art acquired through asset purchases is not disclosed during prosecution, as both unenforceability and antitrust liability may proceed to trial.

impacts.caseName: Seagen Inc. v. Daiichi Sankyo Company, Ltd.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an antibody-drug conjugate patent claiming a tetrapeptide linker with intracellular cleavage satisfies written description and enablement requirements when the specification provides limited guidance on the full scope of tetrapeptide variants.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed a $41+ million jury verdict in favor of Seagen, holding that the asserted claims of its ADC patent were invalid for lack of written description and enablement. The court found the specification did not adequately describe the full scope of claimed tetrapeptide linker variants or enable a person of ordinary skill to make and use the claimed invention without undue experimentation. This overturned findings of willful infringement against Daiichi Sankyo.
impacts.positiveImplications: Pharmaceutical and biotech companies accused of patent infringement benefit from heightened scrutiny of broad genus claims in complex biological inventions, particularly for ADC technologies.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the life sciences face stricter written description and enablement standards for broad claims covering large numbers of variants, potentially undermining valuable patent portfolios in the ADC space.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming methods and systems for a server that tailors content delivery specifications to a requesting wireless device's screen size and capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court and held that all asserted claims across three related patents were ineligible under § 101, requiring entry of judgment for Netflix. The court found that tailoring content delivery specifications to device capabilities was an abstract idea and that the claims lacked an inventive concept sufficient to transform them into patent-eligible subject matter. The $2.5 million jury verdict for GoTV was reversed.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies facing patent assertions related to adaptive content delivery and device-tailored streaming gain a strong precedent for § 101 invalidity defenses.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the digital media and content delivery space face heightened risk that claims directed to server-side content adaptation will be deemed abstract and patent-ineligible.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to web conferencing systems with time-shifting capabilities for asynchronous viewing of data streams are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed dismissal of a patent infringement action against Google, holding that the web conferencing patent claims were ineligible under § 101. The claims, which covered systems allowing participants to view recorded portions of multimedia presentations while live sessions continued, were found to be directed to an abstract idea without a sufficient inventive concept.
impacts.positiveImplications: Large technology companies operating video conferencing and collaboration platforms benefit from continued narrowing of patent eligibility for software-implemented communication features.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent assertion entities and inventors holding patents on web conferencing and multimedia collaboration technologies face increased difficulty enforcing such patents under § 101.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Board governing discretionary denials of inter partes review institution constitute legislative rules requiring notice-and-comment rulemaking under APA § 553.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions on IPR non-institution discretion are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking. The court reasoned that since Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution, and the Director retains ultimate authority over institution decisions, the instructions do not create binding norms requiring formal rulemaking procedures.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's continued flexibility to decline IPR institution without being constrained by formal rulemaking requirements, preserving patent stability.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies like Apple that rely on IPR to challenge patents asserted against them face continued uncertainty about IPR institution, with limited ability to challenge the PTO's discretionary non-institution framework.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Board correctly construed the claim term 'encryption information' in a patent covering partly encrypted adaptive bitrate streaming media content.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the Board's claim construction in an IPR involving DivX's streaming media encryption patent, vacated the Board's decision rejecting Netflix's obviousness challenge, and remanded for further proceedings. The court adopted a different construction of 'encryption information' that could affect the obviousness analysis, potentially invalidating the patent claims.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming media companies like Netflix gain a favorable claim construction that may lead to invalidation of patents covering encrypted adaptive bitrate streaming technologies.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the digital rights management and encrypted streaming space face risk that narrower claim constructions will expose their patents to prior art challenges on remand.

impacts.caseName: Game Plan, Inc. v. Uninterrupted IP, LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether common law trademark rights acquired through an asset purchase agreement can establish priority for purposes of cancelling a registered mark under Section 2(d) of the Lanham Act.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the TTAB's cancellation of Game Plan's registered mark 'I AM MORE THAN AN ATHLETE' and dismissed Game Plan's opposition to UNIP's applications. UNIP established priority through common law rights to 'MORE THAN AN ATHLETE' acquired via a 2019 asset purchase agreement that transferred both the mark and associated goodwill. Game Plan's failure to submit evidence at trial was also fatal to its claims.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies acquiring trademark rights through asset purchases benefit from confirmation that properly structured assignments transferring goodwill can establish priority sufficient to cancel competing registrations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trademark registrants face cancellation risk from competitors who acquire prior common law rights through asset purchases, even after the registrant has already filed opposition proceedings.

impacts.caseName: In re Bayou Grande Coffee Roasting Co.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the TTAB erred in applying the doctrine of foreign equivalents to refuse registration of 'KAHWA' for cafés and coffee shops based on its meaning as Kashmiri green tea.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the TTAB's refusal to register 'KAHWA' for cafés and coffee shops, finding the Board's genericness and mere descriptiveness findings unsupported. The court scrutinized the application of the doctrine of foreign equivalents and whether evidence supported treating 'KAHWA' as generic or merely descriptive based on its meaning as a type of Kashmiri tea not shown to be sold in American cafés.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses using foreign-language terms as brand names benefit from the court's rigorous evidentiary requirements before the doctrine of foreign equivalents can bar trademark registration.

impacts.caseName: Coda Development S.R.O. v. Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether Goodyear was entitled to judgment as a matter of law overturning a $64 million jury verdict for trade secret misappropriation related to self-inflating tire technology.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's grant of JMOL in favor of Goodyear, overturning a jury verdict of $2.8 million in compensatory and $61.2 million in punitive damages for trade secret misappropriation. The court also affirmed denial of correction of inventorship. The decision addressed the sufficiency of evidence for trade secret claims involving complex technology collaboration between companies.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies engaged in technology collaboration benefit from rigorous evidentiary standards for trade secret misappropriation claims, providing protection against large jury awards when evidence of misappropriation is insufficient.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trade secret owners face high evidentiary burdens to sustain misappropriation verdicts, particularly in cases involving complex technology and collaborative development relationships.

impacts.caseName: Secretary of Defense v. Pratt & Whitney
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an agreement between Pratt & Whitney and the government regarding the inclusion of 'Drag' costs in the overhead pool for cost-plus government contracts was valid and enforceable.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit addressed cost allocation disputes in cost-plus defense contracts between Pratt & Whitney and the government. The court dismissed part of the appeal for lack of a final decision on overhead base allocation but held that the purported agreement on including 'Drag' in the overhead pool was invalid. Drag costs—calculated as a fixed percentage of collaborator revenue rather than actual overhead—were at the center of the dispute over proper cost allocation under Cost Accounting Standards.
impacts.positiveImplications: The government benefits from invalidation of cost allocation arrangements that inflate overhead pools charged to cost-plus contracts, potentially reducing defense procurement costs.
impacts.negativeImplications: Defense contractors using collaboration agreements with revenue-sharing structures face risk that resulting cost calculations may be deemed inconsistent with Cost Accounting Standards, potentially requiring repayment of overcharges.

impacts.caseName: Wonderland Switzerland AG v. Evenflo Company, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly found infringement of car seat patents under the doctrine of equivalents and whether the permanent injunction was properly scoped.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed in part, reversed in part, and vacated in part a judgment finding Evenflo infringed Wonderland's car seat patents. The court addressed infringement under the doctrine of equivalents, the scope of permanent injunctive relief, and willful infringement. The injunction was stayed pending appeal as to one patent, highlighting the court's scrutiny of injunction scope in patent cases.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in the consumer products space benefit from affirmation of doctrine of equivalents infringement findings and permanent injunctive relief.
impacts.negativeImplications: Product manufacturers face injunction risk even where literal infringement is not established, and the court's partial reversal and vacatur signal limits on overbroad injunctive relief.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations is consistent with the governing statute and the common law right of access.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of International Trade's ruling that the ITC's blanket confidentiality practice for questionnaire responses is incompatible with the statute governing confidential treatment of business information in trade proceedings. The court held that 19 U.S.C. § 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial records and that confidentiality designations must be made on a case-by-case basis consistent with statutory requirements.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers and domestic producers benefit from increased transparency in trade proceedings, as automatic blanket confidentiality can no longer shield all questionnaire responses from public access.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies submitting commercially sensitive data in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations face increased risk of disclosure, as the ITC must now evaluate confidentiality on a case-by-case basis rather than applying blanket protections.

impacts.caseName: Willis Electric Co., Ltd. v. Polygroup Ltd. (Macao Commercial Offshore)
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly denied JMOL of obviousness and a new trial on damages for a patent covering pre-lit artificial trees with integrated electrical connections.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the denial of Polygroup's JMOL on obviousness and motion for a new trial on damages for Willis's patent on pre-lit artificial trees with simultaneous mechanical and electrical connections. The decision upholds the validity and enforceability of patents covering consumer product innovations in the holiday decoration market.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in consumer products benefit from affirmation that incremental but commercially significant innovations in product assembly and design can withstand obviousness challenges.
impacts.negativeImplications: Competitors in the artificial tree and holiday decoration market must navigate valid patents covering integrated electrical connection features, limiting design freedom.
